3.why: An empty list has nothing to reverse — detecting this now avoids unnecessary work.
3.invariant: No values have been collected yet.

4.happening: Assigning result = [] for the empty-input path.
4.why: We need a variable to return; the reverse of an empty list is an empty list.

5.why: No nodes exist, so the answer is trivially an empty list.
5.invariant: result is correct for the empty case.

6.happening: Building head_links = {head_links} — the sequential link array.
6.why: The linked list renderer needs explicit next-pointers to draw arrows between nodes. Node i points to i+1; the last node points to -1 (null).
6.invariant: head_links mirrors the original forward chain; no values collected yet.

7.happening: Creating values = [] to store node values in forward order.
7.why: We need to remember every value before we can reverse them.
7.invariant: values is empty; no values have been collected yet.

8.happening: Setting pos = 0 — pointing at the first node.
8.why: pos is our walking index; we start at the beginning of the list.

9.happening: pos ({pos}) < {len(head)} — still more nodes to visit. Entering the collection loop.
9.why: We visit every node in forward order to collect all values.
9.invariant: values holds the first {len(values)} node values; head[0..{pos}-1] collected.

9.happening: pos ({pos}) >= {len(head)} — all {len(head)} nodes visited. Exiting the collection loop.
9.why: Every node's value has been saved into values.
9.invariant: values = {values} — all node values collected in forward order.

10.why: Saving this node's value so we can reverse it later.

11.why: Moving to the next node — like following a next-pointer in a real linked list.
11.invariant: All nodes before index {pos+1} have been collected.

12.happening: Initialising result = [] — the list that will hold reversed values.
12.why: We need a new container to build the answer; values holds the forward order.
12.invariant: result is empty; values = {values} is complete.

13.happening: Setting i = {len(values)-1} — the index of the last element in values.
13.why: Reading back-to-front produces the reverse; we start at the rightmost element.
13.invariant: i points at the last (rightmost) element of values.

14.happening: i ({i}) >= 0 — still elements to reverse. Entering the reversal loop.
14.why: We keep reading from the back of values until we've read every element.
14.invariant: result holds the reverse of values[{i}+1..end]; {len(result)} elements reversed so far.

14.happening: i ({i}) < 0 — all elements reversed. Exiting the reversal loop.
14.why: Every element has been appended to result in reverse order.

15.why: Reading from the back of values reverses the order into result.

16.why: Moving one step left — the next element to read is one index closer to the front.
16.invariant: Everything in values[{i}..end] has been added to result.

17.why: result now contains all node values in reversed order — that is the answer.
17.invariant: result is the complete reverse of the input.

init.happening: Setting up the link array, values collector, and pos index.
init.why: We need explicit next-pointers for the renderer, a storage container for values, and a walking index.

check.happening: Checking whether the list is empty.
check.why: An empty input requires no work.

loop.why: Deciding whether there are more elements to process.
loop.invariant: All previous elements have been handled.

update.why: Storing the current element's contribution to the result.

return.why: All values have been collected and reversed; the answer is ready.
